long_reaction_chains.name: Long Reaction Chains
long_reaction_chains.description: Questions involving multiple sequential steps, reagents, or transformations.

structural_patterns: Product of one step becomes reactant of next

counting_enumeration.name: Counting/Enumeration
counting_enumeration.description: Questions requiring counting of molecules, ions, bonds, or specific numerical calculations.

deep_mechanistic_reasoning.name: Deep Mechanistic Reasoning
deep_mechanistic_reasoning.description: Questions requiring understanding of reaction mechanisms, intermediates, or transition states.

comparative_ranking.name: Comparative Ranking
comparative_ranking.description: Questions requiring comparison of properties (acidity, basicity, reactivity, etc.) and arranging in orders.

questions.question_text: Which one of the following first row transition elements is expected to have the highest third ionization enthalpy? (A) Iron (Z=26) (B) Manganese (Z=25) (C) Chromium (Z=24) (D) Vanadium (Z=23)
questions.solution_summary: Mn (Z=25) has electronic configuration [Ar]3d5 4s2. After removing two electrons (4s2), Mn2+ has stable d5 configuration. Removing a third electron (from d5) requires high energy. Hence, Mn has the highest third ionisation enthalpy.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires comparing electronic configurations of transition metals and understanding the stability of half-filled d5 configuration.

questions.question_text: Which of the following compounds will give a ketone on oxidation with chromic anhydride (CrO3)? (A) (CH3)2CH-CH2OH (B) CH3CH2CH2OH (C) (CH3)3C-OH (D) CH3-CH2-CH(OH)-CH3
questions.topic: Organic Chemistry - Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
questions.solution_summary: Secondary alcohols on oxidation with CrO3 give ketones. CH3-CH2-CH(OH)-CH3 is a secondary alcohol (OH attached to carbon bearing two alkyl groups).

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires identifying primary, secondary, and tertiary alcohols and their oxidation products.

questions.question_text: Two among the three components of DNA are β-D-2-deoxyribose and a heterocyclic base. The third component is: (A) Adenine (B) Phosphoric acid (C) Sulphuric acid (D) Uracil
questions.solution_summary: DNA is composed of three components: a nitrogenous base (adenine, guanine, cytosine, or thymine), a pentose sugar (β-D-2-deoxyribose), and phosphoric acid.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Pure recall of the components of DNA.

questions.question_text: For an electrolyte undergoing association in a solvent, the van't Hoff factor: (A) is always greater than one (B) has negative value (C) has zero value (D) is always less than one
questions.solution_summary: When an electrolyte undergoes association (dimerisation or polymerisation), the number of particles decreases, making the van't Hoff factor (i) less than 1.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding the effect of association on the van't Hoff factor.

questions.question_text: For the reaction X + 2Y → P, the differential form equation of the rate law is: (A) 2d[P]/dt = -d[Y]/dt (B) -d[P]/dt = -d[X]/dt (C) +d[X]/dt = -d[P]/dt (D) -2d[Y]/dt = +d[P]/dt
questions.solution_summary: For the reaction X + 2Y → P, the rate of disappearance of Y is twice the rate of disappearance of X and twice the rate of formation of P. Thus, -d[Y]/dt = 2 × d[P]/dt, or -2d[Y]/dt = +d[P]/dt.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding the relationship between rates of disappearance and appearance based on stoichiometry.

questions.question_text: The compound which undergoes SN1 reaction most rapidly is: [Image with options]
questions.solution_summary: SN1 reaction rate depends on carbocation stability. Benzylic and tertiary halides form highly stable carbocations via resonance/hyperconjugation and react fastest.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires comparing carbocation stability to determine SN1 reactivity.

questions.question_text: Acetic acid reacts with PCl5 to give: (A) Cl-CH2-COCl (B) Cl-CH2-COOH (C) CH3-COCl (D) CCl3-COOH
questions.topic: Organic Chemistry - Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids
questions.solution_summary: Carboxylic acids react with PCl5 to form acyl chlorides (acid chlorides). Acetic acid (CH3COOH) gives acetyl chloride (CH3COCl).

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires knowledge of the reaction of carboxylic acids with PCl5.

questions.question_text: The formation of cyanohydrin from an aldehyde is an example of: (A) nucleophilic addition (B) electrophilic addition (C) nucleophilic substitution (D) electrophilic substitution
questions.topic: Organic Chemistry - Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids
questions.solution_summary: Cyanohydrin formation is a nucleophilic addition reaction where CN- (nucleophile) attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon of the aldehyde, followed by protonation.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires identifying the type of reaction based on the mechanism of cyanohydrin formation.

questions.question_text: In the Arrhenius equation, when log k is plotted against 1/T, a straight line is obtained whose: (A) slope is A/R and intercept is Ea (B) slope is A and intercept is -Ea/R (C) slope is -Ea/RT and intercept is log A (D) slope is -Ea/2.303R and intercept is log A
questions.solution_summary: Arrhenius equation in logarithmic form: log k = log A - (Ea/2.303R)(1/T). So, slope = -Ea/2.303R and intercept = log A.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires recalling the logarithmic form of the Arrhenius equation and the slope/intercept relationship.

questions.question_text: The reaction of an alkyl halide with sodium alkoxide forming ether is known as: (A) Wurtz reaction (B) Reimer-Tiemann reaction (C) Williamson synthesis (D) Kolbe reaction
questions.topic: Organic Chemistry - Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
questions.solution_summary: Williamson synthesis is the reaction of an alkyl halide with a sodium alkoxide to form an ether.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Pure recall of the name of the reaction for ether synthesis.

questions.question_text: The correct order of the ease of dehydration of the following alcohols by the action of conc. H2SO4 is: (A) (CH3)3C-OH > (CH3)2CH-OH > CH3CH2-OH (B) (CH3)2CH-OH > CH3CH2-OH > (CH3)3C-OH (C) CH3CH2-OH > (CH3)2CH-OH > (CH3)3C-OH (D) (CH3)2CH-OH > (CH3)3C-OH > CH3CH2-OH
questions.topic: Organic Chemistry - Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
questions.solution_summary: Dehydration of alcohols proceeds via carbocation formation. Ease of dehydration follows carbocation stability: tertiary > secondary > primary.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires comparing carbocation stability to determine the ease of dehydration of alcohols.

questions.question_text: Which functional groups of glucose interact to form cyclic hemiacetal leading to pyranose structure? (A) Aldehyde group and hydroxyl group at C-4 (B) Aldehyde group and hydroxyl group at C-5 (C) Ketone group and hydroxyl group at C-4 (D) Ketone group and hydroxyl group at C-5
questions.solution_summary: The pyranose ring of glucose is formed by the reaction between the aldehyde group at C1 and the hydroxyl group at C5.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires recalling the formation of the pyranose ring in glucose.

questions.question_text: Assertion (A): When NaCl is added to water a depression in freezing point is observed. Reason (R): NaCl undergoes dissociation in water.
questions.solution_summary: Assertion is true: NaCl lowers the freezing point of water. Reason is true and explains it: NaCl dissociates into Na+ and Cl- ions, increasing the number of particles, which causes greater freezing point depression.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of colligative properties and the effect of dissociation.

questions.question_text: Assertion (A): Separation of Zr and Hf is difficult. Reason (R): Zr and Hf have similar radii due to lanthanoid contraction.
questions.solution_summary: Assertion is true: Zr and Hf are difficult to separate. Reason is true and explains it: due to lanthanoid contraction, Zr and Hf have almost identical ionic radii, making their chemical properties very similar.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of lanthanoid contraction and its consequences.

questions.question_text: Assertion (A): The pKa of ethanoic acid is lower than that of Cl-CH2-COOH. Reason (R): Chlorine shows electron withdrawing (-I) effect which increases the acidic character of Cl-CH2-COOH.
questions.topic: Organic Chemistry - Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids
questions.solution_summary: Assertion is false: Cl-CH2-COOH is stronger acid (lower pKa) than ethanoic acid. Reason is true: Cl is electron withdrawing, stabilising the carboxylate ion and increasing acidity.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of substituent effects (inductive effect) on acidity of carboxylic acids.

questions.question_text: Assertion (A): Aniline is a stronger base than ammonia. Reason (R): The unshared electron pair on nitrogen atom in aniline becomes less available for protonation due to resonance.
questions.solution_summary: Assertion is false: aniline is weaker base than ammonia. Reason is true: the lone pair on nitrogen in aniline is delocalised into the benzene ring via resonance, making it less available for protonation.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of resonance effect on basicity of aromatic amines.

questions.question_text: Calculate the potential of Iron electrode in which the concentration of Fe2+ ion is 0.01 M. (E°Fe2+/Fe = -0.45 V at 298 K) [Given: log 10 = 1]
questions.solution_summary: Applying the Nernst equation for the Fe2+/Fe electrode.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires application of the Nernst equation for a metal-metal ion electrode.

questions.question_text: Define molecularity of the reaction. State any one condition in which a bimolecular reaction may be kinetically of first order.
questions.correct_answer: Molecularity is the number of reacting species (atoms, ions, or molecules) that must collide simultaneously in an elementary reaction. A bimolecular reaction may become first order when one reactant is present in large excess (pseudo first order condition).
questions.solution_summary: Definition of molecularity and condition for pseudo first order.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Pure recall of the definition of molecularity and pseudo first order condition.

questions.question_text: What happens when D-glucose is treated with the following reagents? (a) HI (b) Conc. HNO3
questions.correct_answer: (a) Glucose + HI → n-hexane (reduction). (b) Glucose + conc. HNO3 → Saccharic acid (oxidation of both aldehyde and primary alcohol groups).
questions.solution_summary: Reactions of glucose with HI (reduction) and conc. HNO3 (oxidation).

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires recall of specific reactions of glucose with HI and conc. HNO3.

questions.question_text: (a) Draw the structures of major monohalo products in each of the following reactions: [Image: (i) CH=CH2 + HBr, (ii) UV light halogenation]. (b) Give reasons for the following: (i) Grignard reagent should be prepared under anhydrous conditions. (ii) Alkyl halides give alcohol with aqueous KOH whereas in the presence of alcoholic KOH, alkenes are formed.
questions.correct_answer: (a)(i) Markovnikov addition of HBr to alkene gives the more substituted alkyl bromide. (ii) Free radical halogenation gives the most stable radical product. (b)(i) Grignard reagents react with water (and other protic solvents) to form alkanes, so they must be prepared under anhydrous conditions. (ii) Aqueous KOH provides OH- for substitution (SN2) to give alcohol; alcoholic KOH provides strong base for elimination to give alkene.
questions.solution_summary: Products of halogenation and reasons for anhydrous conditions and different products with KOH.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires drawing products and understanding mechanistic reasons for reactivity.

questions.question_text: Write the chemical equation when: (a) Butan-2-one is treated with Zn(Hg) and conc. HCl. (b) Two molecules of benzaldehyde are treated with conc. NaOH.
questions.topic: Organic Chemistry - Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids
questions.correct_answer: (a) CH3COCH2CH3 + Zn(Hg)/HCl → CH3CH2CH2CH3 (butane) - Clemmensen reduction. (b) 2C6H5CHO + NaOH → C6H5CH2OH + C6H5COONa - Cannizzaro reaction.
questions.solution_summary: Clemmensen reduction of ketone and Cannizzaro reaction of benzaldehyde.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires recalling Clemmensen reduction and Cannizzaro reaction.

questions.question_text: When a certain conductivity cell was filled with 0.05 M KCl solution, it has a resistance of 100 ohm at 25°C. When the same cell was filled with 0.02 M AgNO3 solution, the resistance was 90 ohm. Calculate the conductivity and molar conductivity of AgNO3 solution. (Given: Conductivity of 0.05 M KCl solution = 1.35 × 10^-2 ohm^-1 cm^-1)
questions.solution_summary: Using the cell constant from KCl solution to find conductivity and molar conductivity of AgNO3.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires application of cell constant, conductivity, and molar conductivity formulas.

questions.question_text: The following initial rate data were obtained for the reaction: 2NO(g) + Br2(g) → 2NOBr(g). [Table: Expt 1: [NO]=0.05, [Br2]=0.05, Rate=1.0×10^-3; Expt 2: [NO]=0.05, [Br2]=0.15, Rate=3.0×10^-3; Expt 3: [NO]=0.15, [Br2]=0.05, Rate=9.0×10^-3]. (a) What is the order with respect to NO and Br2? (b) Calculate the rate constant (k). (c) Determine the rate of reaction when [NO]=0.4M and [Br2]=0.2M.
questions.sub_topic: Determination of Order from Initial Rate Data
questions.correct_answer: (a) When [Br2] triples (0.05 to 0.15) keeping [NO] constant, rate triples → order w.r.t Br2 = 1. When [NO] triples (0.05 to 0.15) keeping [Br2] constant, rate increases 9 times → order w.r.t NO = 2. Overall order = 3. (b) Rate = k[NO]^2[Br2]. k = 1.0×10^-3 / (0.05^2 × 0.05) = 8 L^2 mol^-2 s^-1. (c) Rate = 8 × (0.4)^2 × 0.2 = 0.256 mol L^-1 s^-1.
questions.solution_summary: Analysis of initial rate data to determine orders, rate constant, and rate at given concentrations.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires analysis of initial rate data to determine orders and calculate rate constant and rate.

questions.question_text: (a) Write the formula for the following coordination compound: Potassium tetrahydroxidozinate (II). (b) Arrange the following complexes in the increasing order of conductivity of their solution: [Cr(NH3)5Cl]Cl2, [Cr(NH3)3Cl3], [Cr(NH3)6]Cl3. (c) Identify the type of isomerism exhibited by the following complexes: (i) [Co(NH3)5NO2]^2+ (ii) [Co(en)3]Cl3
questions.correct_answer: (a) K2[Zn(OH)4]. (b) Increasing conductivity: [Cr(NH3)3Cl3] (neutral, 0 ions) < [Cr(NH3)5Cl]Cl2 (2 ions) < [Cr(NH3)6]Cl3 (3 ions). (c)(i) Linkage isomerism (NO2 vs ONO). (ii) Optical isomerism.
questions.solution_summary: Formula writing, conductivity order based on number of ions, and identification of isomerism types.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires applying IUPAC rules, understanding conductivity based on ionization, and identifying isomerism types.

questions.question_text: (a) Which of the following is an allylic halide? (i) CH3-CH=CH-Br (ii) CH2=CH-CH(Br)-CH3. (b) Out of chlorobenzene and 2,4,6-trinitrochlorobenzene, which is more reactive towards nucleophilic substitution and why? (c) Which isomer of C4H9Cl has the lowest boiling point?
questions.correct_answer: (a) (ii) CH2=CH-CH(Br)-CH3 is allylic halide (Br attached to carbon adjacent to C=C). (b) 2,4,6-Trinitrochlorobenzene is more reactive due to electron withdrawing nitro groups stabilizing the carbanion intermediate. (c) tert-Butyl chloride (2-chloro-2-methylpropane) has the lowest boiling point due to maximum branching.
questions.solution_summary: Identification of allylic halide, reason for enhanced reactivity of nitro-substituted chlorobenzene, and lowest boiling point isomer.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of allylic halides, effect of substituents on reactivity, and factors affecting boiling points.

questions.question_text: (a) Write the mechanism of the following reaction: CH3CH2OH →[H+] CH2=CH2. (b) Write the main product in each of the following reactions: [Image: (i) Hydroboration-oxidation of but-1-ene, (ii) Oxidation of ketone]
questions.topic: Organic Chemistry - Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers / Aldehydes, Ketones
questions.correct_answer: (a) Mechanism: Step 1: Protonation of -OH. Step 2: Loss of water to form carbocation (rate-determining). Step 3: Deprotonation to form ethene. (b)(i) Hydroboration-oxidation of but-1-ene gives butan-1-ol (anti-Markovnikov). (ii) Oxidation of ketone gives carboxylic acids (cleavage).
questions.solution_summary: Mechanism of acid-catalysed dehydration and products of hydroboration-oxidation and ketone oxidation.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires writing the mechanism for dehydration and predicting products of hydroboration-oxidation and oxidation.

questions.question_text: Answer the following: (any three) (a) What is peptide linkage? (b) What type of bonds hold a DNA double helix together? (c) Which one of the following is a polysaccharide? Sucrose, Glucose, Starch, Fructose. (d) Give one example each for water-soluble vitamins and fat-soluble vitamins.
questions.correct_answer: (a) Peptide linkage: -CO-NH- bond between amino acids. (b) Hydrogen bonds. (c) Starch is a polysaccharide. (d) Water-soluble: Vitamin C (or B-complex). Fat-soluble: Vitamin A (or D, E, K).
questions.solution_summary: Definitions and identification of biomolecules.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires recall of definitions and identification of biomolecules.

questions.question_text: Compound (A) (C6H12O2) on reduction with LiAlH4 gives two compounds (B) and (C). The compound (B) on oxidation with PCC gives compound (D) which upon treatment with dilute NaOH and subsequent heating gives compound (E). Compound (E) on catalytic hydrogenation gives compound (C). The compound (D) is oxidized further to give compound (F) which is found to be a monobasic acid (Molecular weight = 60). Identify the compounds (A), (B), (C), (D), (E) and (F).
questions.topic: Organic Chemistry - Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids
questions.correct_answer: A = CH3COOC2H5 (ethyl acetate). B = C2H5OH (ethanol). C = CH3OH (methanol). D = CH3CHO (acetaldehyde). E = CH3CH=CHCHO (crotonaldehyde). F = CH3COOH (acetic acid, MW=60).
questions.solution_summary: Stepwise identification: ester hydrolysis/reduction, oxidation of alcohol to aldehyde, aldol condensation, hydrogenation, and further oxidation to acetic acid.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires connecting multiple reactions to deduce the identity of unknown compounds in a sequence.

questions.question_text: Case: Batteries and Fuel Cells. (a) How do primary batteries differ from secondary batteries? (b) The cell potential of Mercury cell is 1.35 V, and remains constant during its life. Give reason. (c) Write the reactions involved in the recharging of the lead storage battery. OR (c) Write two advantages of fuel cells over other galvanic cells.
questions.correct_answer: (a) Primary batteries are not rechargeable; secondary batteries are rechargeable. (b) The overall reaction in a mercury cell does not involve any change in ion concentration, so the potential remains constant. (c) Recharging of lead storage battery: Cathode: PbSO4 + 2e- → Pb + SO4^2-. Anode: PbSO4 + 2H2O → PbO2 + SO4^2- + 4H+ + 2e-. OR (c) Advantages of fuel cells: (1) Higher efficiency (about 70%) than ordinary cells. (2) Pollution-free (only water as by-product).
questions.solution_summary: Differences between primary and secondary batteries, reason for constant potential of mercury cell, and reactions/advantages.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of battery types, mercury cell chemistry, and fuel cell advantages.

questions.question_text: Case: Crystal Field Theory. (a) What is crystal field splitting energy? (b) Give reason for the violet colour of the complex [Ti(H2O)6]^3+ on the basis of crystal field theory. (c) [Cr(NH3)6]^3+ is paramagnetic while [Ni(CN)4]^2- is diamagnetic. Explain why. [Atomic No.: Cr=24, Ni=28] OR (c) Explain why [Fe(CN)6]^3- is an inner orbital complex, whereas [Fe(H2O)6]^3+ is an outer orbital complex. [Atomic No.: Fe=26]
questions.correct_answer: (a) Crystal field splitting energy is the energy difference between t2g and eg orbitals in an octahedral field. (b) Ti3+ is d1 (t2g^1 eg^0). d-d transition from t2g to eg absorbs light, giving the complementary colour (violet). (c) Cr3+ is d3 (t2g^3), paramagnetic. Ni2+ in [Ni(CN)4]^2- is d8, CN- is strong field, all electrons paired → diamagnetic. OR (c) CN- is strong field, pairs electrons → d2sp3 (inner orbital). H2O is weak field, no pairing → sp3d2 (outer orbital).
questions.solution_summary: CFT definitions, explanation of colour, and magnetic/inner-outer orbital classification.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires deep understanding of CFT, d-d transitions, and magnetic properties of complexes.

questions.question_text: (a) (i) At the same temperature, CO2 gas is more soluble in water than O2 gas. Which one of them will have higher value of KH and why? (ii) How does the size of blood cells change when placed in an aqueous solution containing more than 0.9% (mass/volume) sodium chloride? (iii) 1 molal aqueous solution of an electrolyte A2B3 is 60% ionized. Calculate the boiling point of the solution. (Given: Kb for H2O = 0.52 K kg mol^-1) OR (b) (i) The vapour pressures of A and B at 25°C are 75 mm Hg and 25 mm Hg, respectively. If A and B are mixed such that the mole fraction of A in the mixture is 0.4, then calculate the mole fraction of B in vapour phase. (ii) Define colligative property. Which colligative property is preferred for the molar mass determination of macromolecules? (iii) Why are equimolar solutions of sodium chloride and glucose not isotonic?
questions.correct_answer: (a)(i) O2 has higher KH because KH is inversely proportional to solubility. (ii) Blood cells shrink (crenation) due to osmosis. (iii) A2B3 → 2A^3+ + 3B^2-, n=5. α=0.6, i=1+(n-1)α = 1+4×0.6 = 3.4. ΔTb = i × Kb × m = 3.4 × 0.52 × 1 = 1.768 K. Tb = 373 + 1.768 = 374.768 K. OR (b)(i) PT = 75×0.4 + 25×0.6 = 45 mm Hg. yB = (25×0.6)/45 = 15/45 = 0.333. (ii) Colligative property depends on number of solute particles. Osmotic pressure is preferred for macromolecules. (iii) NaCl dissociates (i=2), glucose does not (i=1), so they have different osmotic pressures.
questions.solution_summary: Application of Henry's law, osmosis, boiling point elevation, Raoult's law, and colligative properties.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires application of multiple concepts: Henry's law, osmosis, boiling point elevation, van't Hoff factor, Raoult's law, and colligative properties.

questions.question_text: Answer any five of the following: (a) N,N-diethyl-benzenesulphonamide is insoluble in alkali. Give reason. (b) Aniline does not undergo Friedel-Crafts reaction. Why? (c) Write a simple chemical test to distinguish between methylamine and aniline. (d) Write the chemical reaction involved in Gabriel phthalimide synthesis. (e) How will you convert aniline to p-bromoaniline? (f) Complete the following reaction: [Image: Diazonium salt reaction]. (g) Write the structures of A and B in the following reaction: COOH + NH3 → A →[Br2+NaOH] B.
questions.correct_answer: (a) No H atom on N, so not acidic. (b) Aniline forms salt with AlCl3 (Lewis acid catalyst), deactivating the ring. (c) Carbylamine test: methylamine gives foul smell; aniline gives dye test. (d) Phthalimide + KOH → Potassium phthalimide + RX → N-alkylphthalimide + H2N-NH2 → Primary amine. (e) Aniline + Br2 in acetic acid → p-bromoaniline. (f) Product of diazonium reaction. (g) A = C6H5CONH2 (benzamide), B = C6H5NH2 (aniline) via Hofmann degradation.
questions.solution_summary: Reasons for insolubility, failure of Friedel-Crafts, distinguishing tests, and reactions.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of amine chemistry, including tests, reactions, and conversions.

questions.question_text: (a) (i) Account for the following: (1) The melting and boiling points of Zn, Cd and Hg are low. (2) Of the d4 species, Cr2+ is strongly reducing while Mn3+ is strongly oxidizing. (3) E° value of Cu2+/Cu is +0.34 V. (ii) Complete and balance the following chemical equations: KMnO4 →[heat]; Cr2O7^2- + 6I^- + 14H+ →. OR (b) (i) Out of Cu2Cl2 and CuCl2, which is more stable in aqueous solution and why? (ii) Write the general electronic configuration of f-block elements. (iii) Predict which of the following will be coloured in aqueous solution and why? Sc3+, Fe3+, Zn2+. [Atomic number: Sc=21, Fe=26, Zn=30] (iv) How can you obtain potassium dichromate from sodium chromate? (v) Why do transition metals and their compounds show catalytic activities?
questions.topic: Inorganic Chemistry - d- and f-Block Elements
questions.correct_answer: (a)(i)(1) Zn, Cd, Hg have completely filled d10 configuration, weak metallic bonding, low m.p./b.p. (2) Cr2+ → Cr3+ (stable d3); Mn3+ → Mn2+ (stable d5). (3) Cu has high atomisation enthalpy and low hydration enthalpy. (ii) 2KMnO4 → K2MnO4 + MnO2 + O2; Cr2O7^2- + 6I^- + 14H+ → 2Cr3+ + 3I2 + 7H2O. OR (b)(i) CuCl2 is more stable (Cu2+ more stable due to high hydration enthalpy). (ii) (n-2)f^1-14 (n-1)d^0-1 ns^2. (iii) Fe3+ is coloured (d5, unpaired electrons); Sc3+ (d0) and Zn2+ (d10) are colourless. (iv) 2Na2CrO4 + H2SO4 → Na2Cr2O7 + Na2SO4 + H2O; Na2Cr2O7 + 2KCl → K2Cr2O7 + 2NaCl. (v) Due to variable oxidation states and ability to form complexes.
questions.solution_summary: Reasons for periodic trends, redox behaviour, balancing equations, and transition metal properties.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires deep understanding of transition metal chemistry, periodic trends, and redox behaviour.
